The script `battery_status_helper.sh` is designed to write an arbitrary value provided as a command-line argument to all CPU scaling governor files under `/sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu*/cpufreq/scaling_governor`. While controlling CPU governors is a legitimate administrative task, this script does so without any validation or access controls.

The critical issue is that it accepts an unvalidated user input (`$1`) and writes it directly into a privileged system interface. Writing an invalid or unsupported governor name could destabilize the system or cause performance issues. More importantly, this script runs with elevated privileges (as it must write to root-owned sysfs files), and accepting untrusted input creates a privilege escalation vector. An unprivileged user who can invoke this script could potentially misuse it to disrupt system operation, especially in environments where this script is invoked via a service or helper without proper input sanitization.

Although it may appear as a simple administrative utility, the lack of input validation combined with privileged sysfs access makes this script a security risk.
</details>

## battery_status_helper.sh

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Unvalidated input written to sysfs with root privileges

Details:

The script `battery_status_helper.sh` is designed to write an arbitrary value provided as a command-line argument to all CPU scaling governor files under `/sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu*/cpufreq/scaling_governor`. While controlling CPU governors is a legitimate administrative task, this script does so without any validation or access controls.

The critical issue is that it accepts an unvalidated user input (`$1`) and writes it directly into a privileged system interface. Writing an invalid or unsupported governor name could destabilize the system or cause performance issues. More importantly, this script runs with elevated privileges (as it must write to root-owned sysfs files), and accepting untrusted input creates a privilege escalation vector. An unprivileged user who can invoke this script could potentially misuse it to disrupt system operation, especially in environments where this script is invoked via a service or helper without proper input sanitization.

Although it may appear as a simple administrative utility, the lack of input validation combined with privileged sysfs access makes this script a security risk.
